words: SAM SCHRAGER: I was thinking to start with that maybe you'd tell some general
background about how the settlement of this area and the people that came
here were like and related to what happened other places in the Northwest.
LOLA GAMBLE CLYDE: All right, I'll start in on the piece about how this fits
in to the general settlement of the Northwest. And that's all right. That's
a good way to introduce It because it pinpoints it in history for people
who never heard of Moscow, Idaho before. Then I'll come down to these
different stories about how Moscow was named. Do you want me to do that?

speaker: SAM
words: And I think perhaps mention^fie first people that came through here and

speaker: SAM
words: what traces they left. That's very interesting too.

speaker: LC
words: Um hum, urn hum.Yeah fine.

speaker: SAM
words: So go ahead.

speaker: LC
words: Well, the history of our community,Sam, parallels the history of

speaker: LC
words: Pacific followed by the fur seekers,

speaker: LC
words: the whole Northwest. We too had the coming of the explorers filbwed

speaker: LC
words: by the great soul seekers, followed by the great gold seekers, followed

speaker: LC
words: by some form of temporary government, and then lastly followed by the home

speaker: LC
words: seekers--the people who would stay and build the churches and the homes and

speaker: LC
words: the schools of the community. We know that in 1805 and 1806 Lewis and Clark

speaker: LC
words: passed this way. And their diary tells us that they camped along the

speaker: LC
words: Clearwater River, and that their hunters ranged for about a hundred miles.

speaker: LC
words: So beyond a doubt they came to Tatkinmah , which was what this

speaker: LC
words: lovely valley was called by the Nez Perce Indians^meaning "the place of the

speaker: LC
words: little spotted deer." Here they hunted for the deer in the winter and dug

speaker: LC
words: camas here in the summer. These were followed by the fur seekers. And we

speaker: LC
words: know that Donald MacKenzie built a fur trading post along the north bank

speaker: LC
words: of the Clearwater River. And undoubtedly his fur trappers cam up the

speaker: LC
words: Tenatpanep which is the south Palouse and Paradise Creek looking

speaker: LC
words: for the silver fur of the beaver. And we can see the beaver yet digging

speaker: LC
words: along those creeks in the early spring. They left no written record of

speaker: LC
words: having passed this way. And we know/die great soul seeker, Henry Harmon

speaker: LC
words: Spalding rode this way from tapwai, the place of the white butterfly, the place of

speaker: LC
words: many springs, twenty-five miles out of Spokane.

speaker: LC
words: But he drew no map and no where in his diary does he comment about having

speaker: LC
words: ridden this way, Sut it's in a direct line and beyond a doubt he passed

speaker: LC
words: this way.

speaker: LC
words: Following the soul seekers came of course the first white men to

speaker: LC
words: visit this territory and leave a written record of having passed this way.

speaker: LC
words: At that was Isaac Stevens, the first governor of Washington Territory.

speaker: LC
words: And on May 24th, 1855 he met in the lush meadows of Walla Walla to trade

speaker: LC
words: with the Northwest Indians. He came directly from there through this

speaker: LC
words: country. And with him was a great German artist and cartographer, Gustavus

speaker: LC
words: Sohon and he drew amap of the way they came And they wrote ln their

speaker: LC
words: diaries about having passed this way. We know that about the west side of

speaker: LC
words: Paradise Creek, on the 20th of June, they arrived there and camped overnight.

speaker: LC
words: And Isaac Infill Stevens wrote in his report:"You could look away to the west,

speaker: LC
words: and the ground was just carpeted with a myriad of flowers." they stayed

speaker: LC
words: overnight and followed down the slope of Paradise Ridge to cross the

speaker: LC
words: Tanatpan the South Palouse about at the place where the Chinese gardens

speaker: LC
words: now stand. And he wrote then in his diary that along the banks of the creek

speaker: LC
words: there were six hundred ledges of Nez PerceSencamped. And that the camas

speaker: LC
words: was so thick that the women in three or four days could dig enough camas

speaker: LC
words: roots to last them all winter long. And

speaker: LC
words: and this would of course be Indian Hills and University Height:— grazed

speaker: LC
words: two thousand head of Nez Perce ponies. And of course among them would be

speaker: LC
words: many of

speaker: LC
words: the lovely Appaloosa horses for which Moscow was to become international

speaker: LC
words: headquarters. And he a^wrote in his diary at this time: "Here I was astonished

speaker: LC
words: at the luxuriousness of the grass and at the richness of the soil!"

speaker: LC
words: Following the coming of Isaac Stevens we have the coming of the gold

speaker: LC
words: seekers. And we know that g^n. Pierce passed this way on the way to Canal

speaker: LC
words: Gulch where gold was discovered in 1860. He was lead by Jane Silcott;who

speaker: LC
words: was the daughter of old chief Timothy.The E. D.Pierce party had come by way

speaker: LC
words: of Walla Walla, had camped overnight with Timothy^at the Red Wolf Campground.

speaker: LC
words: Here the Indians told them that it wasn't safe to go through the Nez Perce

speaker: LC
words: country, ffiey were resenting the coming of so many white people. So Pierce

speaker: LC
words: asked if there was any way around this country. And Timothy's daughter,

speaker: LC
words: little Jane later became Jane Silcott, seventeen at the time, said

speaker: LC
words: well she knew cl way around and she would lead the company. So coming

speaker: LC
words: up Steptoe Canyon, they came through halfway between Colton and Uniontowny

speaker: LC
words: and came over as far as the iouth Palouse. And they followed the iouth

speaker: LC
words: Palouse through what is now Troy, down to Kendrick, up over the

speaker: LC
words: Leland country to Canal Gulchjwhere gold was discovered. And this of course

speaker: LC
words: meant wagons west ^henthe cry of gold went ou^. Almost overnight, Lewisto^

speaker: LC
words: which had just been a meeting place for the Nez Perce Indians^ became a

speaker: LC
words: tent city of ten thousand people This of course had to lead to some

speaker: LC
words: form of

speaker: LC
words: of temporary government.

speaker: LC
words: In 1863, Idaho became a territory. And this of

speaker: LC
words: course was followed by the coming of the first white settlers to our

speaker: LC
words: community. There had been cattle people here, but they had only been

speaker: LC
words: temporarily; there'd been gold seekers through, there'd been mining people

speaker: LC
words: who set up temporary shacks. In 1870 came Tom who was the first

speaker: LC
words: white man to settle in Latah County, down along the Thorn Creek way, almost

speaker: LC
words: on the Washington side. He set up the first homestead. In 71 came the

speaker: LC
words: first covered wagons in to the of Moscow. In that first group were

speaker: LC
words: Lieuallen and his family, the Haskins family Mrs. Lieuallen

speaker: LC
words: was a Haskins) the George Washington Tomers-, came Bill Frazier and

speaker: LC
words: his family, Cameron and his brother. Daniel came that same year.

speaker: LC
words: The William Taylors--he was considered one of the founding fathers of

speaker: LC
words: Moscow-settled out along the South Palouse where the Latah County Nursing

speaker: LC
words: Home now stands. He soon established a large garden and planted out trees.

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: people while they looked for other homesteads to settle in this community.

speaker: LC
words: There are many stories about how Moscow got itl name and one thing

speaker: LC
words: about folklore-there are as many ways telling it as there are people

speaker: LC
words: to do the telling. But we do know that the Indians had called it Tatkinmah

speaker: LC
words: meaning "the place of the young deer." The first settlers noticed what

speaker: LC
words: good food the camas roots were for the Indian people. They were nourishing and

speaker: LC
words: they grew fat on it. They thought it might be a good place to raise hogs.

speaker: LC
words: So many of the men brought in hogs in the early days, and because the hogs

speaker: LC
words: too prospered and grew fat on the camas bulbs, they started calling the

speaker: LC
words: place Hog Heaven. Weli with the coming of the settlers wives and the wagon

speaker: LC
words: trains in to the vicinity they thought Hog Heaven was a poor address. You

speaker: LC
words: couldn't very well wiite borne and tell 'em you were living in Hog Heaven.

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: Paradise. And Paradise Hill and Paradise Creek and Paradise Villa still

speaker: LC
words: bear that name. And there are many other stories about how it was named.

speaker: LC
words: And one is that Asbury Almon Lieuallen, who was considered one of Moscow's

speaker: LC
words: first settlers.had a cow that got out at night and ate up one of the

speaker: LC
words: neighbor^ flower gardens. And the lady was very upset about it the next

speaker: LC
words: morning. But Mr. Lieuallen said, "Well now you can't blame it on me

speaker: LC
words: because it was Ma's cow that did that. So the name became Moscow. And the

speaker: LC
words: other story is that some of the founding fathers were sitting in a barn

speaker: LC
words: trying to think up what they would call the new settlement and the cow

speaker: LC
words: stuck her head through the window. Somebody said, "Well, there's a cow

speaker: LC
words: with moss on her back, so we'fl name thtplace Moscow."But those I thiik

speaker: LC
words: were just folklore.

speaker: SAM
words: What about the story that said there wereRussian settlers, wasn't there one

speaker: SAM
words: that said that too?

speaker: LC
words: Yes. Sone people thought that there might have been Russian settlers,

words: But I held. Brosnan who has an authority on Idaho history research it one

speaker: LC
words: time, and we could find no Russian names on any of the tax records or in

speaker: LC
words: any of the ear ly day voting records in the county. So we finally wrote to

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: his father had been postmaster when the name was changed from Paradise

speaker: LC
words: Valley to Moscow. And he said that his father, Mr. Neff, had come from a little

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: moved to a place called Moscow in Iowa. He thought that his father had

speaker: LC
words: called it Moscow, Idaho, remembering the two towns where he had once lived.

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: the news in the early part, in the early years of the 1800s. It was the

speaker: LC
words: time of Napoloon's historic retreat through the snow and the blizzards of

speaker: LC
words: the Russian country. And there are about sixteen towns altogether in the

speaker: LC
words: United States named Moscow, and most of them wernamed for Moscow, Russia

speaker: LC
words: in the early part of the 1800's. (Break) Father Cataldo writes J in

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: north of where Moscow is, but Henry Harmon Spalding never mentions it.

speaker: SAM
words: his way to someplace else?

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: would have been a good resting place like overnight', but I don't suppose

speaker: LC
words: there were enough people here to stop and talk religion to. nobody here.

speaker: SAM
words: 95 as being the place that ; they stopped? Do you know how they ever figured

speaker: SAM
words: that out?

speaker: LC
words: Well, there*8 a spring out there in what they call Section 9. And I think

speaker: LC
words: that that was how they did it. There's a spring there, and in Father

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: no use in marking a spring that's way off the highway where no one can

speaker: LC
words: see it. The cross and the marker were pi on the highway where people could. and know

words: met.

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: Paradise Butte which would be. what we call through the Blaine country, and

speaker: LC
words: came down about -where the Red Wolf marker is.

speaker: LC
words: And that was the greater Nez Perce frail. Now the Red Wolf Trail came

speaker: LC
words: up from the Red Wolf Campground;which was at Alpowa. And that was Old

speaker: LC
words: Timothy's home; and he was Spalding's first Christian convert. And he

speaker: LC
words: called this place Alpowa because it was derived from words that sounded

speaker: LC
words: that way that meant "the place of Sabbath rest." Old Timothy had early

speaker: LC
words: adopted the ways of the missionaries, and he wouldn't let anyone work on

speaker: LC
words: Sunday So he called his place Alpwa, meaning the "place of Sabbath rest."

speaker: LC
words: thrtftigtt

speaker: LC
words: And the Red Wolf Trail ran from '.h there up what is now called Steptoe

speaker: LC
words: Canyon, and it: ;came up throuigt'horn Creek. It crossed the divide and

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: put it there to mark the meeting place of the Nez Perce Trail with the

words: Red Wolf Trail.

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

words: would go that way.

speaker: LC

words: that the railroads cross the United States.And of Indians had followed

speaker: LC
words: the game trails, the early game trails of the Northwest.

speaker: SAM

speaker: LC
words: straight

speaker: LC
words: the river,they just came/up the on across.

speaker: SAM

words: county? How close do you think they really came? Do you think they

speaker: SAM

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: been here, but we just think if they were out hunting that it's quite

speaker: LC
words: likely that their hunters and trappers ranged this far. But they left no

speaker: LC
words: written record to prove that.

speaker: SAM

speaker: SAM

speaker: LC
words: Well, we have the written record of Isaac IngallStevens that there were

speaker: LC
words: about six hundred lodges camped here. And he was the first white mop. that

speaker: LC
words: left any record of having been here. 4nd then it was in June, and of

speaker: LC
words: course the camas would just be going out of bloom. And you could easily

speaker: LC
words: find the bulbs. And they wereigging the bulbs and beyond atioubt they

speaker: LC

words: they used this country as a hunting ground and as a camas digging ground.

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

words: they'd take their horses along to fatten them up.If it was on a

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC

speaker: LC
words: evidences that the archeologists could Cite to you of them having been here.

speaker: LC

words: times they hm together.

words: many

words: they

words: of the Ed Snow place, and I heard him tell that  come in and the

words: any staying in it; they just put it up as a safeguard in case they needed

words: it.

words: and a place to sleep

speaker: SAM
words: have food and watTer's and all that in the stockade. I imagine it would

speaker: LC

words: out and get food and bring it in to them and so on, but. . .
